title: Modal Verbs — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: Basic + expanded + perfect modals + speculation — the Band 7+ nuance toolkit
intro: Modals carry meaning that no other word can: ability, possibility, necessity, advice, deduction, criticism. Band 6 candidates use only can / must / should — and only in the present. Band 7+ candidates use the full range, including PERFECT MODALS (must have, should have, could have) for past speculation and regret. Perfect modals are the single highest-value modal feature for Band 7.5+.

sections.title: 1. The Modal System — Form Basics
sections.summary: Modals share key grammar properties. Get these right; the rest is meaning.

rules.pattern: Modal grammar rules — the same for all modals
items: Followed by BARE infinitive (no 'to'): 'I can swim' (NOT 'I can to swim')
No -s on third person: 'She can swim' (NOT 'She cans')
No 'do/does/did' for questions or negatives: 'Can you swim?' / 'I cannot swim' (NOT 'Do you can swim?')
Negative formed by adding 'not': can't, won't, shouldn't, mustn't, mightn't, couldn't, wouldn't
Modals do NOT have infinitive (*to can) or -ing (*canning) forms. Use replacement phrases.

rules.pattern: Modal replacements — when modals don't have a form

examples.plural: I want to be able to (not 'to can') swim.
I will have to (not 'will must') leave early.
I had to (not 'musted') leave early.
I will be able to (not 'will can') help.

rules.note: The 'be able to / have to' alternatives are NOT modals — they conjugate normally.

rules.pattern: Modal + perfect — the 'past' form for present modals

examples.plural: He MUST be tired now. → He MUST HAVE BEEN tired yesterday.
You SHOULD apologise. → You SHOULD HAVE apologised yesterday.
She COULD help us today. → She COULD HAVE HELPED us yesterday.

rules.note: Modal + have + V3 = the modal meaning applied to a past situation. This is the Band 7+ payoff.

sections.title: 2. Ability — Can / Could / Be Able To
sections.summary: Three forms with subtle differences for present, past, and future ability.

rules.pattern: CAN — present ability

examples.plural: She can speak four languages.
Children can learn languages quickly.

rules.pattern: COULD — past general ability

examples.plural: When I was young, I could run very fast.
She could read by the age of four.

rules.pattern: WAS / WERE ABLE TO — past specific success

examples.plural: Despite the rain, we WERE ABLE TO finish the match.
He WAS ABLE TO solve the puzzle in 5 minutes.

rules.note: Critical distinction: 'could' = general past ability; 'was able to' = specific past success on one occasion. ❌ 'I could finish the test yesterday' is wrong if you mean 'I succeeded'. ✔ 'I was able to finish the test yesterday.'

rules.pattern: WILL BE ABLE TO — future ability

examples.plural: After the course, students WILL BE ABLE TO present in English.
By 2050, AI WILL BE ABLE TO perform many manual tasks.

rules.pattern: COULDN'T — past inability (both general and specific)

examples.plural: She couldn't swim until she was ten.
I couldn't open the door — it was locked.

rules.note: Negative is simpler — 'couldn't' works for both general and specific past inability.

sections.title: 3. Permission & Requests — Can / May / Could / Would
sections.summary: From most informal to most formal.

rules.pattern: Asking permission

examples.plural: Can I borrow your pen?
Could I borrow your pen?
May I borrow your pen?
Might I trouble you for your pen?

rules.pattern: Asking someone to do something

examples.plural: Can you help me?
Could you help me?
Would you mind helping me?
Would you be so kind as to help me?

rules.pattern: Granting permission

examples.plural: You can take a break now.
Students may use the library after 8 p.m.

sections.title: 4. Obligation, Necessity & Prohibition
sections.summary: Must / have to / should / ought to / had better — each has a slightly different shade.

rules.pattern: MUST — strong obligation (often from speaker's authority)

examples.plural: I must finish this report tonight.
All applicants must submit the form by Friday.
You must read this book — it's brilliant!

rules.pattern: HAVE TO — external obligation (rules, circumstances)

examples.plural: We have to wear uniforms at school.
I have to leave early — my train is at 6.

rules.note: Subtle difference: 'must' often = I/we/the speaker says so; 'have to' = the rules/circumstances say so. Both common; both correct.

rules.pattern: MUSTN'T vs DON'T HAVE TO — opposite meanings

examples.plural: You mustn't smoke here. (= it's forbidden)
You don't have to come if you don't want to. (= it's not necessary)

rules.note: Critical Band 7+ distinction. 'You mustn't' and 'You don't have to' are NOT the same.

rules.pattern: SHOULD / OUGHT TO — advice, recommendation

examples.plural: You should drink more water.
Governments should invest more in renewable energy.
The bus should arrive any minute now.
We ought to address this issue urgently.

rules.pattern: HAD BETTER — strong advice with implied warning

examples.plural: You had better leave now, or you'll miss the train.
You had better not be late.

rules.note: Bare infinitive after 'had better' (not 'to'). Negative: 'had better NOT'. Stronger than 'should'.

sections.title: 5. Deduction & Speculation — Modal Strength Scale
sections.summary: Rank modals by certainty for present and past speculation. This is one of the highest-yield Band 7+ areas.

rules.pattern: PRESENT speculation — strength scale

examples.plural: The lights are on. He must be home.
He can't be at home — his car isn't there.
He might be at the gym. / She may be in the library.

rules.note: ❌ Common error: 'mustn't be' for negative deduction. The negative of deductive 'must' is 'can't' / 'couldn't'.

rules.pattern: PRESENT speculation — continuous form (action in progress)

examples.plural: She isn't answering. She must be sleeping.
He might be working late tonight.
She can't be working — she's on holiday.

rules.pattern: PAST speculation — perfect modals (THE BAND 7+ MARKER)

examples.singular: MUST HAVE V3 (strong past deduction — positive)
examples.plural: The road is wet. It must have rained last night.

examples.singular: CAN'T / COULDN'T HAVE V3 (strong past deduction — negative)
examples.plural: She can't have stolen the money — she was with me all evening.

examples.singular: COULD / MAY / MIGHT HAVE V3 (50% — past possibility)
examples.plural: He might have forgotten our appointment.
She may not have received the email.

rules.note: Drilling perfect modals is THE single highest-value modal exercise. Most Band 6 candidates can't form 'must have been' or 'might have forgotten' under pressure.

rules.pattern: Past continuous speculation

examples.plural: He looked tired. He must have been working all night.
She didn't see me. She might have been thinking about something else.

sections.tip: Speaking Part 2 cue cards about people you don't know well are perfect for speculation: 'I imagine she must be very disciplined; she might also be quite reserved in interviews.'

sections.title: 6. Regret, Criticism & Hypothetical Past — Perfect Modals
sections.summary: Use perfect modals to express regret about your past or criticism of others' past actions. Hugely under-used by Band 6 candidates.

rules.pattern: SHOULD HAVE V3 — past advice / regret / criticism

examples.plural: I should have studied harder for the exam.
You should have called me earlier.
Governments should have acted on climate change decades ago.
I shouldn't have ignored the warning signs.

rules.pattern: COULD HAVE V3 — unrealised past possibility

examples.plural: I could have gone to the meeting, but I chose not to.
She could have won the prize, but she didn't enter.
Many lives could have been saved with earlier intervention.

rules.pattern: WOULD HAVE V3 — third conditional / past hypothetical

examples.plural: If I had known, I would have helped.
I would have called, but my phone died.

rules.pattern: MIGHT HAVE V3 — past possibility (lower certainty)

examples.plural: She might have left already.
Without the new policy, the situation might have worsened.

rules.pattern: NEEDN'T HAVE V3 — unnecessary past action (BrE)

examples.plural: You needn't have brought a gift — but thank you.
We needn't have hurried — the meeting was postponed.

rules.note: Different from 'didn't need to V': 'needn't have V3' = action was done but wasn't necessary; 'didn't need to V' = action wasn't necessary (might or might not have been done).

sections.tip: In Speaking Part 2 about a memory or decision, drop in ONE perfect modal: 'I should have taken the offer' / 'It could have gone differently' / 'I might have missed the train if I hadn't run.' This single sentence signals Band 7+.

sections.title: 7. Modals in IELTS Writing & Speaking
sections.summary: Each part of IELTS has high-yield modal patterns.

rules.pattern: Writing Task 2 — recommendations and warnings

examples.plural: Governments should invest more in renewable energy.
Stricter regulations should be implemented.
This issue must be addressed before it worsens.
Such measures could significantly reduce inequality.

rules.pattern: Writing Task 2 — academic hedging with modals

examples.plural: Such policies may have unintended consequences.
This trend could pose a serious threat to traditional industries.

rules.pattern: Writing Task 2 — perfect modals in evaluation

examples.plural: Many of these problems should have been anticipated.
Earlier intervention could have prevented the crisis.
Without strict regulation, the situation would have worsened considerably.

rules.pattern: Speaking Part 2 — describing a person

examples.plural: I imagine she must be very disciplined.
He might be in his late thirties — I'm not sure.

rules.pattern: Speaking Part 3 — hypothetical and impersonal

examples.plural: Governments could do more by investing in technology.
This kind of policy might face resistance from older generations.
Schools should teach financial literacy from an early age.

sections.tip: Audit your last Task 2 essay: count modal verbs. Aim for at least 6–8 different modals across 250 words, including at least ONE perfect modal (should have / could have / might have).

sections.title: 8. Top Modal-Verb Errors at the Band 6 → 7+ Threshold
sections.summary: Specific mistakes that pull Band 7 candidates down to 6.5.

rules.pattern: 1. 'To' after a modal

examples.plural: ✔ I can swim.
✔ You should study harder.

rules.pattern: 2. -s on third person modal

examples.plural: ✔ She can speak French.
✔ He must leave.

rules.pattern: 3. 'Mustn't' confused with 'don't have to'

examples.singular: ❌ You mustn't come if you're busy. (intended: it's not necessary)
examples.plural: ✔ You don't have to come if you're busy. (= no obligation)

examples.singular: ✔ You mustn't smoke here. (= it is forbidden)
examples.plural: Different meanings entirely.

rules.pattern: 4. 'Could' for specific past success

examples.singular: ❌ I could finish the test yesterday.
examples.plural: ✔ I was able to / managed to finish the test yesterday.

rules.note: 'Could' for specific past success is wrong unless negative ('couldn't finish').

rules.pattern: 5. 'Mustn't' for negative deduction

examples.singular: ❌ He mustn't be at home — his car isn't here.
examples.plural: ✔ He can't be at home — his car isn't here.

rules.pattern: 6. Wrong perfect modal form

examples.plural: ✔ He must have GONE home. (V3, not V2)

examples.singular: ❌ I should have studied. is it 'studied' or 'study' or 'studying'?
examples.plural: ✔ I should have STUDIED. (modal + have + V3)

rules.pattern: 7. Trying to conjugate modals

examples.singular: ❌ I will can help you tomorrow.
examples.plural: ✔ I will be able to help you tomorrow.
✔ I have had to leave.

rules.pattern: 8. Using 'may' for ability instead of permission/possibility

examples.plural: ✔ I can speak three languages. (ability = can)
(permission = may)

sections.tip: Make a personal modal-perfect drill card: 'must have / should have / could have / might have / can't have / would have / shouldn't have'. Practise saying each + a verb in V3 form daily for 2 weeks. After this, perfect modals become automatic.

checklist: I never put 'to' after a modal verb (can swim, NOT can TO swim).
I never put -s on third-person modals (she can, NOT she cans).
I distinguish 'mustn't' (forbidden) from 'don't have to' (not necessary).
I use 'was/were able to' (not 'could') for SPECIFIC past success.
I use 'can't / couldn't' (not 'mustn't') for negative present deduction.
I can form modal + have + V3 correctly (must have gone, should have studied, might have forgotten).
I deploy at least one perfect modal in any Speaking Part 2 about a memory or past situation.
I use should/must/can in active AND passive forms (must be addressed, can be implemented).
I use 'will be able to / will have to' instead of trying to conjugate modals into the future.
I use modal hedging (may, might, could) in Task 2 to soften absolute claims.
I never write *to can / *will must / *musting / *have musted.
